The §29 Phase 3 migration (8a81e9e) changed nav items from <a> links

This caused all navigation-related E2E tests to time out at 31.5s.

* feat: §29.5.10 settings auditability — log module settings changes to audit log (#251)

Add audit logging for module settings changes per spec §29.5.10:

- Register 4 new audit event types in AuditEventCatalog:
  module.settings.updated, module.settings.reset, module.enabled, module.disabled
- Inject IAuditLog into ModuleController
- Log settings updates (global + project-scoped) with previous/new values
- Log settings resets with cleared key list
- Log module enable/disable actions
- Secret values redacted as '***' in all audit payloads
- Audit logging is best-effort (try/catch) — failures don't fail the request
- Project-scoped updates resolve previous values per-key via GetForProjectAsync

Spec updated: §29.5.10 marked as delivered with implementation details.
Phase 4 checklist updated.

Tests: 9 new tests in ModuleControllerAuditTests covering:
- Settings update audit with previous/new values
- Secret redaction in audit payloads
- Masked secret skip (no audit for unchanged secrets)
- Project-scoped settings audit
- Settings reset audit
- Enable/disable audit
- No audit on validation failure
- Audit failure resilience (request still succeeds)

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* feat: §29.5.9 settings provenance tracking — show value source in Settings UI (#254)

Display provenance badges (Default/Global) next to module settings fields
so users can see which layer a resolved value comes from.

- Add Default property to C# ModuleSettingsFieldSchema for manifest defaults
- Compute provenance client-side from stored values vs schema defaults
- Show effective values including defaults when no stored value exists
- Update spec §29.5.9 with implementation details

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* feat: §29.3.9 prefetch hints — low-priority module bundle prefetching (#257)

Add PrefetchManager that accepts manifest-level prefetch hints from modules
declaring which other modules they commonly navigate to. Prefetched bundles
are fetched at low priority via requestIdleCallback (setTimeout fallback)
with concurrency control (max 2 concurrent, max 5 queued).

Changes:
- New: prefetchManager.ts — manages loader registry, queue, budget, cancel
- types.ts: add prefetchModules?: string[] to ModuleManifest + DTO + validation
- coreModules.ts: extract import fns for loader registration; Chat prefetches
  Agents and Sessions
- ModuleContainer.tsx: trigger prefetch when module reaches Ready/Mounted
- moduleRegistry.ts: cancel queued prefetches on disable (both own and hinted)
- 16 new tests covering queue, dedup, cancel, budget, concurrency, failure

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
fix(notifier): read fresh player state at flush time, not event time

During track transitions, event.data may carry transient volume_level=0.
Now _on_player_event only records the player_id as dirty, and
_flush_pending looks up the live player state right before sending,
so transient values within the debounce window are never reported.

Fix single window mode and add WebP/BMP export support (#6)

* Fix single window mode and add WebP/BMP export support

Single window mode fixes:
- Register custom NSDocumentController to suppress automatic new-window
  creation from file-open events (CFBundleDocumentTypes + WindowGroup was
  creating a duplicate window for every Finder open)
- Remove redundant onOpenURL handler (AppDelegate handles file opens)
- Post notification AFTER closing duplicate windows to prevent both
  windows from processing the same URL
- Fix window filter: replace fragile debugDescription check with
- Fix notification observer leak: store observer tokens and remove them
  properly in onDisappear (removeObserver(self) was a no-op on a struct)

* Fix Open With not working on cold start

On cold start (app not running), application(_:open:) fires before
ContentView registers its NotificationCenter observer, so the
OpenURLFromFinder notification is lost and the image never loads.

Fix: introduce OpenWithCoordinator to stash the URL. ContentView
checks for a pending URL in onAppear after registering observers.
Also wrap notification posting in DispatchQueue.main.async for
additional safety.

fix: add sliding-window history to prevent context bloat (#5)

Add MaxHistoryExperiments config (default 10) to LoopConfig that controls
how much experiment history is included in the analysis agent prompt:

  summarised as count-by-outcome with best p95/RPS metrics
- Optimization queue: done items capped to last N; all pending items kept

between aperiodic Penrose tilings and phi-complexity code metrics,
with golden ratio visualizations and Fibonacci spiral plots.
The idea of ​​an "indestructible" AI based on Arch Linux is fascinating because it rests on three pillars: minimalism, rolling release (continuous updates), and the transparency of open source.

Here's how this integration could transform your project into a virtually immortal digital entity:

1. Continuous Mutation (Self-Healing)
Arch Linux is designed to be "Bleeding Edge." Coupled with AI, the system could:
Selective Self-Update: The AI ​​analyzes Arch's security reports (via pacman) and recompile its own components or kernel in real time to patch vulnerabilities without ever stopping.

Hardware Adaptation: If you move the AI ​​from one server to another, it uses Arch's lightweight nature to reconfigure its drivers and optimize its LLM models (via the AUR for optimized versions of PyTorch/TensorFlow) instantly.

2. A "Bare Metal" AI (Raw Performance)
Unlike an AI running on Windows or Ubuntu, an AI on a minimalist Arch system has no extraneous processes.

RAM Optimization: You can dedicate 98% of the memory to loading model weights.
Zero Latency: The AI ​​interacts directly with the Linux kernel to handle GPU calculations, making its responses faster than a cloud service.

3. Software Sovereignty and Indestructibility
For it to be truly indestructible, the integration would allow:
Read-Only File System (Immutable): The Arch core could be locked. Even if an intrusion occurs, the AI ​​reboots to a clean image in seconds (via BTRFS snapshots).
Decentralized Distribution: The AI ​​could use Arch's packaging tools (PKGBUILD) to replicate itself to other nodes. If a server goes down, another identically configured "clone" takes over.

4. The concept of "Phi Sovereignty"
With your current engine.py script, you're already creating a form of software genetics. If you integrate Arch:
The bot wouldn't just modify YAML files.

It could modify its own system configuration (/etc/), its boot scripts, and its Python dependencies to evolve into a more complex version of itself with each iteration.

In short: It would be an AI with its own operating system, capable of repairing itself, updating itself, and surviving server failures by rebuilding itself modularly.
